title: Dysoxylum salutare
intro: Dysoxylum salutare, also known as the "Kapok tree," is a species of plant in the family Meliaceae, native to the tropical regions of Asia. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its potential health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and antimicrobial properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of fever, cough, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Dysoxylum salutare include alkaloids, terpenoids, and flavonoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, which are used to treat various health conditions, and it is also used as a component in some herbal remedies for respiratory and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Dysoxylum salutare is classified as a species of plant in the family Meliaceae, commonly known as the mahogany family. This family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants that are found primarily in tropical and subtropical regions around the world. The Meliaceae family includes around 300 species of trees and shrubs, many of which are valued for their timber and medicinal properties. Dysoxylum salutare is a member of the genus Dysoxylum, which consists of around 40 species of flowering plants found primarily in the tropical forests of Asia and the Pacific. The classification of Dysoxylum salutare reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history as a member of this distinct group of plants.
